agent1_snippets.content: Patients in whom symptoms remain refractory to H1-antihistamines should be referred to a dermatologist and/or clinical immunologist for review and initiation of second and third-line therapeutic agents. CSU follows a fluctuant disease course and may resolve spontaneously. Accordingly, efficacy and need for continuation of treatment should be evaluated periodically at 3- to- 6-month intervals. [1] The psychological burden of CSU should not be underestimated, and where appropriate, counseling and psychotherapy may enhance overall health outcomes. [27]

agent1_snippets.title: Omalizumab -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Omalizumab received initial approval from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in 2003 for the treatment of moderate-to-severe asthma. Subsequently, in 2014, the drug was also approved for use in patients with chronic idiopathic or spontaneous urticaria in the United States and Europe. Omalizumab is a recombinant humanized immunoglobulin G1 (IgG1) monoclonal anti-IgE antibody utilized to treat allergic asthma and chronic urticaria. This medication works by interacting with the high-affinity receptor Fc-epsilon-RI, typically found on eosinophils, mast cells, and basophils, therefore playing a critical role in preventing the allergic cascade. As a result, omalizumab plays a vital role in managing moderate-to-severe IgE-mediated asthma and, more recently, in treating chronic urticaria. This activity will comprehensively cover the indications, contraindications, mechanisms of action, pharmacokinetics, adverse effects, warnings, precautions, and monitoring requirements of omalizumab. In addition, this activity will also provide updates on some of the critical studies implemented primarily for asthma and include information on urticarial reactions.

agent1_snippets.title: Chronic Urticaria -- Pearls and Other Issues
agent1_snippets.content: Disease duration of less than and more than 6 weeks separates acute from chronic spontaneous urticaria. If the patient cannot recall the time course of urticarial lesions, drawing around an individual lesion with a skin marking pen is useful to document resolution or migration within 24 hours. Where history is unrevealing, an external cause is highly unlikely to be identified for patients with chronic spontaneous urticaria. Diagnostic workup is unrevealing. Skin prick test is not indicated in chronic spontaneous urticaria. Wheals that are painful rather than pruritic, that persist for greater than 24 hours, and/or resolve with ecchymosis or pigmentation should prompt consideration of urticarial vasculitis. The recommended stepwise approach to management begins with second-generation H1-antihistamines, followed by the addition of omalizumab, and subsequently ciclosporin. The rate of spontaneous remission in the first twelve months is as high as 80%.

agent1_snippets.title: Omalizumab -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Omalizumab is a recombinant humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody that specifically targets human immunoglobulin E (IgE). This action prevents the drug's interaction with the high-affinity Fc-epsilon-RI receptor, typically found on eosinophils, mast cells, and basophils. This interaction plays a critical role in the allergic cascade. [1] Omalizumab is vital in managing moderate-to-severe asthma mediated by IgE and, more recently, treating chronic urticaria.

agent1_snippets.title: Omalizumab -- Administration -- Chronic Urticaria
agent1_snippets.content: The recommended dosage for omalizumab for treating chronic urticaria is either 150 or 300 mg  every 4 weeks, depending on the patient's initial serum total IgE level or weight. A retrospective chart review of 43 chronic urticaria patients who received omalizumab between 2006 and 2015 revealed that 37 out of 41 patients (90.2%) responded positively within 3 months duration. [14] Joint guidelines by the American Academy of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology (AAAAI)/American College of Allergy, Asthma, and Immunology (ACAAI) indicate that omalizumab can improve the quality of life, reduce the need for oral corticosteroids, and potentially decrease the need for surgery in chronic urticaria patients. [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Solar Urticaria -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Since chronically exposed areas seem relatively tolerant to sun radiations, phototherapy (UVA, UVB, visible light) and photochemotherapy (PUVA) have been used to induce tolerance (hardening) to sunlight. This hardening process should be based on the spectrum of action and the minimal urticarial dose. PUVA seems to give a longer-lived response than phototherapy alone. Intravenous immunoglobulins, plasma exchange, cyclosporine, afamelanotide, and omalizumab have been used in patients with refractory solar urticaria with some success.

agent1_snippets.title: Golimumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: Continuous interaction between the prescribing specialist and PCP should be maintained with their patient's care. The treatment team should also be regularly updated with the latest guidelines on their patient's disease management. The interprofessional team should thoroughly educate and counsel patients on golimumab. Patients should be aware of any potential adverse effects that may occur and be advised to report any new onset unsuspected symptoms. Golimumab administration should be performed in a healthcare setting where the interprofessional care team is prepared for immediate adverse effects such as hypersensitivity reactions (hives, pruritus, dyspnea, nausea, angioedema, urticaria, anaphylaxis). In any severe events such as anaphylaxis, the treatment should be immediately stopped, and appropriate management should be initiated.

agent1_snippets.title: Solar Urticaria -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: By analogy with idiopathic chronic urticaria treatment, second-generation antihistamine one receptor antagonist agents are largely used and are generally accepted as first-line. These include loratadine, fexofenadine, and cetirizine. They may induce relief in most cases but usually require higher doses, two to four times higher than the conventional ones. 2nd generation antihistamine 1 receptor antagonists seem to have greater efficacy in delayed onset urticaria but no effect on the erythema in solar urticaria.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 690
agent2_snippets.content: Fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorisation indicates that each of these agents is used as a systemic therapy for conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, when inhaled treatments are insufficient. Although they share a common therapeutic class, the drugs have distinct mechanisms of action: fenspiride is a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator; benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway to reduce eosinophilic inflammation; and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), a key upstream cytokine in airway inflammation. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their role as systemic options for managing obstructive airway diseases, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with severe or refractory disease.

The H1‑histamine antagonist class (antagonisté histaminu H1) further includes the compounds triprolidine, eprozinol and protopine, reflecting their shared mechanism of blocking histamine H1 receptors. Together, these relationships highlight a therapeutic landscape in which biologic agents (benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, tezepelumab) and small‑molecule drugs (amlexanox, eprozinol) are grouped for the management of obstructive airway diseases, while certain small molecules also possess antihistaminic activity that may contribute to symptom control.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 416
agent2_snippets.content: Amlexanox, fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as drugs for obstructive airway diseases. Within this broad category, the ATC hierarchy defines two specialized subgroups: “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” and “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants.” The subgroup “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” is an inverse‑isa of the parent class “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES,” indicating that it is a more specific category under the overall group. The same inverse‑isa relationship exists for the inhalant subgroup, and the inhalant subgroup is also explicitly linked to the parent class by an isa relationship, confirming its status as a direct child of the broader category. Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are members of the systemic subgroup, reflecting that they are administered systemically (e.g., oral, subcutaneous or intravenous routes) for the treatment of conditions such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and other obstructive airway disorders. Fenspiride is listed as a member of both the systemic subgroup and the inhalant subgroup, suggesting that it may be used either systemically or as an inhaled preparation in the management of obstructive airway disease. The inclusion of biologic agents such as benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab highlights the role of targeted monoclonal‑antibody therapies in severe asthma and related airway inflammation, while amlexanox’s placement in the systemic subgroup points to its potential off‑label or investigational use for airway disease. This ATC classification framework clarifies the therapeutic positioning of each drug, aids clinicians in selecting appropriate treatment routes, and supports consistent pharmacovigilance and prescribing practices for patients with obstructive airway diseases.

Other agents in this nasal antiallergic group are antazolin, levocabastine, olopatadine, and the H1‑blocking alkaloid protopine, all of which act as topical antihistamines. Nedokromil is also a child of the generic “N [Preparations]” group and is identified as a histamine H1 antagonist, linking it mechanistically to other H1‑blocking drugs such as triprolidine. Systemic therapy for obstructive airway disease is represented by amlexanox and fenspiride, both members of the ATC class “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” Additional systemic agents in this class include eprozinol, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab.

agent2_snippets.content: Methapyrilen and omalizumab are both assigned to the therapeutic class of substances that affect the respiratory system. As members of this class, they are used to influence breathing function and are relevant in the treatment of respiratory disorders. Recognizing that both drugs belong to the same therapeutic category helps clinicians consider them when selecting pharmacologic strategies aimed at modulating respiratory function.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 697
agent2_snippets.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” category in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. This grouping indicates that each of these agents is used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The list includes several monoclonal antibodies—benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab—that target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, as well as roflumilast, a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that helps control inflammation and bronchoconstriction. Amlexanox, although less commonly used for airway disease, is also placed in this category, reflecting its systemic therapeutic role. Grouping these diverse agents together highlights the range of systemic pharmacologic strategies available for managing obstructive airway diseases and underscores their shared classification despite differing mechanisms of action.

The hierarchy places non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonists for systemic use under the broader class “adrenergics for systemic use”, while non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants belong to the class “adrenergics, inhalants”. Alpha‑ and beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants are also a subclass of “adrenergics, inhalants”. The overarching category “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES” is linked inversely to both “adrenergics, inhalants” and “adrenergics for systemic use”, and it is a subclass of “OTHER DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES, INHALANTS in ATC”.
